**OVERNIGHT MARKET BRIEFING**

**US Markets (Close Mar 26)**
S&P 500 fell 1.74% to 5,477 — on pace for its 5th consecutive weekly loss, the longest streak since 2022. Nasdaq dropped 2.38% (−521 pts), officially entering correction territory at −10% from ATH. Dow shed 469 pts (−1.01%) to 41,960. Russell 2000 down 1.9%. VIX surged 8.9% to 29.88 — fear is elevated but not at panic levels yet.

**Today's Session (Mar 27 early):** S&P futures up 0.39% to 5,550. Dow futures +163 pts (+0.35%). Nasdaq futures +0.40%. Early bounce but fading — markets opened lower, S&P down 0.89% to 5,420, Nasdaq −1.25% to 21,140. Broad-based selloff, tech and growth leading the decline.

**Key Data Today:** PCE inflation 8:30 AM ET (consensus +0.3% MoM core). Hot print could spike yields further. University of Michigan consumer sentiment finalized at 53.3, down 5.8% from Feb — lowest since 2022.

**Treasuries & Rates:** 10-year yield at 4.28%, up 2bps overnight. 30-year approaching 5.0% — major headwind for growth equities. Futures market now pricing 52% probability of a rate INCREASE by year-end — first time crossing 50%. This is a massive shift from the 3-cut consensus just weeks ago.

**Oil & Commodities:** WTI crude at $96.72 (+2.37%). Brent above $110 — 17.8M bbl/day flow through Strait of Hormuz disrupted. Gold steady at $3,048, acting as a haven. Goldman Sachs energy desk has turned more cautious after being the most bullish on the Street.

**FX & International:** BOJ held at 0.50% but hawkish tone pushed USD/JPY to 148.2. China industrial profits fell 2.3% YoY (Jan–Feb). European PMI mixed — Germany manufacturing 48.3 (contraction), services 52.1 (beat). ECB President warned on inflation from energy shock. Asian nations pivoting to coal as Iran war disrupts oil/gas shipments.

**Iran War Update:** Trump extended the deadline for strikes on Iran's energy infrastructure by 10 days to April 6, saying talks are going "very well." Pakistan confirmed it is relaying messages between US and Iranian officials. Iran rejected the initial 15-point peace proposal as a "nonstarter." IDF bombed Iran's most central naval arms production site and ballistic missile factories — strikes in Tehran damaged 82,000 civilian buildings across 20 of 32 provinces. Israel sent a 3rd military division into Lebanon to create a security zone up to Litani river. UAE intercepted 15 ballistic missiles and 11 drones — debris killed 2 in Abu Dhabi. Pentagon considering 10,000 additional ground troops to the Middle East. Senate approved DHS funding bill (excluding ICE) early this morning.

**Suspicious Trading Alert:** Axios reports an epidemic of suspicious trading around Trump's most consequential decisions — each time, just minutes or hours before he rattles global markets, someone places large bets. Senate introduced the Public Integrity in Financial Markets Act to curb insider bets on prediction markets.

Stock now below all key moving averages (SMA-20 $627, SMA-50 $645, SMA-200 $688). Oversold but no floor in sight. Prev close $547.54. [Source](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/META/)


**AMZN (~$202, −2.7%)** — Continued weakness. Oil price spike pressuring shipping cost expectations. Consumer spending trends under pressure from UMich sentiment at 53.3. [Source](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/AMZN/)

**NFLX ($93.54, −0.5%)** — Relatively stable. Raised subscription prices across all tiers for the 4th time in 4 years. Volume remains elevated at ~59M vs avg 37M — institutional repositioning. [Source](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/NFLX/)

**BURL ($323.77, −1.7%)** — Executing "TJX-lite" strategy with smaller footprints, better inventory turnover. Trade-down effect accelerating. Stock was $329 earlier this week. [Source](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/BURL/)

**RDDT ($127.26, −2.1%)** — Still hovering around broken $125 support. CTO and senior leaders selling. Despite Q4 revenue $726M (+70% YoY), leadership confidence eroding. Range today $124–$137. [Source](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/RDDT/)

**PINS ($17.84, −4.5%)** — Weak today. Elliott's $1B activist stake still the catalyst. Pinterest raised Q1 revenue forecast after acquiring CTV ad platform tvScientific. Selloff creates a better entry for the thesis. [Source](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/PINS/)

**DIS** — Market cap ~$181.6B. Watching theme park attendance data and streaming subscriber trends. No major incremental news.

**UBER ($72.44, +0.2%)** — Flat to slightly green. Holding up well despite rising fuel costs. Day range $72.30–$72.97.

**SHOP** — E-commerce sentiment soft amid consumer spending concerns. Trading down with broader tech.

**SPOT** — Streaming sector steady, no material updates.

**SNAP ($4.01, −10.7% yesterday)** — Still reeling from EU Digital Services Act investigation into child safety practices. Formal probe could mean hefty fines and stricter oversight. [Source](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/SNAP/)

**ETSY ($48.23, −1.2%)** — Marketplace dynamics under pressure from macro headwinds.

**W (Wayfair)** — Home furnishing demand soft. No specific catalysts.

**LYFT ($13.24, −0.8%)** — Fuel cost exposure similar to UBER but weaker balance sheet.

**V (Visa)** — Cross-border transaction volumes being watched given geopolitical disruption to travel and trade flows.

**WSM ($180.02, −1.5%)** — Premium home segment under pressure. P/E at 20x, dividend yield 1.4%.

**WMG ($23.89, flat)** — No material updates. Music streaming steady.

**MDB (MongoDB)** — Enterprise software sentiment cautious. No incremental news.

**CART ($36.36, −1.3%)** — Grocery delivery demand steady. Day range $35.72–$36.86.

**DASH ($151.53, −2.0%)** — Delivery economics in focus given fuel costs. Day range $149.22–$155.23.

**LYV ($154.97, −0.6%)** — Live events demand holding. Day range $152.32–$155.96.

**NON-CONSENSUS IDEAS**

**1. Long TJX / BURL / ROST — The trade-down accelerant nobody is pricing**
Consensus is bearish on "consumer" as a monolith, but the off-price channel is the direct beneficiary of consumer stress. UMich at 53.3, inflation expectations rising, and real wages getting squeezed by oil — this is the exact setup where TJX comps accelerate. The market is treating all retail the same. It shouldn't be. TJX just printed $60B in revenue and guided up. This is a vol-adjusted long that works in both a recession and a soft landing.

**2. PINS is mispriced post-Elliott — activist + CTV = re-rating catalyst**
Elliott's $1B stake is not a passive position. They bought tvScientific and raised Q1 guidance. The ad market is shifting to connected TV, and Pinterest is quietly becoming a shoppable-video platform. The Street has it pegged as a dying social app. That's wrong. If Elliott pushes for a strategic review or monetization acceleration, this re-rates to 8–10x forward revenue from the current ~5x.

**3. Short RDDT at $125 broken support — insider selling is the canary**
CTO and senior leaders are selling into a stock that just broke major technical support. Revenue growth of 70% YoY looks great until you realize the company has no pricing power and is entirely dependent on ad macro. In a stagflation regime (which is what the Iran war is creating), ad budgets get cut first. The insider selling tells you the people who know the business best don't believe the multiple.

**4. Oil mean-reversion trade — Brent $110+ is not sustainable if talks progress**
Trump extended the deadline to April 6 and Pakistan is relaying messages. If any ceasefire framework emerges, Brent could gap down $15–20 in days. Goldman's energy desk just turned cautious after being the Street's biggest bull. The options market is pricing tail risk to the upside but ignoring the diplomatic optionality. A short-dated put spread on USO or long VIX calls hedged against an energy unwind could be asymmetric.

**5. The rate-hike pricing is overdone — fade the 52% probability**
Futures crossed 50% on a rate increase by year-end for the first time. This is a sentiment extreme. The Fed has repeatedly signaled patience, and the oil shock is a supply-side event, not demand-driven inflation. If PCE core comes in at consensus or below today, the rate-hike pricing collapses and the long end rallies hard. Two-year notes at these levels are attractive if you think the Fed stays put.

title: Evening Briefing — Friday, March 27, 2026
content:
**Good Evening, Jazzy — Friday, March 27, 2026**

The Dow entered correction territory today, down nearly 800 points, as Iran-Strait of Hormuz fears sent oil screaming past $99 WTI and crushed risk appetite across the board. Fifth straight losing week for the S&P — this is no longer a dip, it's a regime change. Watch Sunday night futures for any Iran deadline developments before Asia opens.

**TODAY'S MARKET WRAP**

**US Markets (Close):** The S&P 500 closed at 6,368.85, down 108 points (-1.67%), hitting a seven-month low. The Nasdaq Composite fell 2.15% to 20,948.36, now 13% below its peak. The Dow Jones dropped 793.47 points (-1.73%) to 45,166.64, officially entering correction territory at 10%+ off its highs. The Russell 2000 sank to 2,493.32, down 43 points (-1.70%). The VIX surged to 27.44, up 8.33%, reflecting elevated fear. Volume was above average — NVDA traded 194M shares (10% above its 3-month avg), AMZN at 55.4M (13% above avg). Breadth was terrible: decliners outnumbered advancers roughly 4:1. Energy was the only sector in green (+2.8%), while Tech (-3.1%), Financials (-2.4%), and Consumer Discretionary (-2.6%) led the selloff.

**What Moved:** Iran-US tensions dominated. Trump extended his deadline for strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure by 10 days (to April 6), but the market didn't take the reprieve at face value — Iran turned back Chinese container vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, and the Pentagon is considering deploying up to 10,000 additional ground troops to the Middle East. This is a supply shock story: 17.8M barrels/day flow through Hormuz. Separately, Amazon fell on AI capex margin pressure concerns. Burford Capital collapsed 45% after a legal reversal. Reddit dropped 5% on a landmark youth social media addiction lawsuit. Consumer sentiment hit its lowest since December.

**Treasuries & Rates:** The 2-year yield settled at 4.02%, the 10-year at 4.465%, and the 30-year at 4.95%. The curve bear-steepened as longer-dated yields rose on inflation expectations from energy costs. The Fed held rates steady this week, still projecting one cut in 2026, but acknowledged increased uncertainty from the Middle East conflict. A Brookings speech on March 26 from a Fed governor noted the Iran situation will worsen inflation with minimal growth impact — a stagflationary setup.

**Oil & Commodities:** WTI crude settled at $99.64 (+5.46%), flirting with $100 for the first time in years. Brent topped $112.57 (+4.22%). Gold pulled back to $4,433.53/oz despite the risk-off tone — stronger dollar weighed. Silver fell to $67.73/oz. Copper held around $6.30/lb.

**After-Hours Movers:** Indices ticked higher in thin after-hours trade — S&P 500 futures +0.43%, Nasdaq 100 +0.35%, Dow +0.50%, Russell 2000 +0.60%. No major earnings catalysts after the bell. The bounce felt mechanical, not conviction-driven.

**Geopolitical Update:** This is the key variable for everything right now. Trump's 10-day deadline extension to April 6 buys time but doesn't reduce tail risk — Iran is actively restricting Hormuz traffic, and the US military posture is escalating, not de-escalating. The 15-point ceasefire proposal to Iran is on the table but Tehran's response has been noncommittal. Meanwhile, tariff crosscurrents continue: China opened probes into US trade practices, the EU advanced a US trade deal with a suspension clause above 15% tariffs and a March 2028 sunset, and 2,000+ companies (Costco, FedEx, etc.) filed lawsuits seeking $130B+ in tariff refunds. My read: the market is pricing a contained conflict, but the tail risk of full Hormuz closure is not priced. That asymmetry matters.

**STOCK BRIEFING — END OF DAY**

**META ($525.72, -3.99%)** — Ugly session. Mega-cap tech bore the brunt of the risk-off rotation. Meta's ad-dependent model gets hit twice here: consumer spending pullback fears from energy inflation plus the broader tech derating. Watching the $500 level — that's the next major support. If it breaks, $475 is in play.

**GOOGL ($280.92, -3.44%)** — Alphabet nearing bear market territory. Search remains resilient but the AI capex narrative is weighing on the whole group. Regulatory overhang from the DOJ antitrust case isn't helping either. This is getting to a valuation level that starts to look interesting if you believe the geopolitical situation stabilizes.


**NFLX ($93.11, -0.23%)** — Relative outperformer. Defensive characteristics of the streaming model showing up in a risk-off tape. Consumers cut discretionary spending before they cancel Netflix. The $90 support level has held multiple tests — it's becoming a meaningful floor.

**TJX ($155.12, -1.55%)** — Off-price retail holding up better than the broader market, which is exactly the playbook. When consumers trade down, TJX benefits. The -1.55% decline is significantly less than the index — that's relative strength.

**BURL ($312.50, -1.91%)** — Similar story to TJX. Burlington's value proposition gets stronger as inflation squeezes middle-income wallets. Watch for any PO/routing disruption updates given the Strait of Hormuz shipping situation — this could actually accelerate the trade-down thesis if supply chains get hit.

**ROST ($213.18, -1.85%)** — Completing the off-price trifecta. Ross hit a new 1-year high on March 26, pulled back modestly today. Relative strength is notable. These three names (TJX/BURL/ROST) are acting as safe havens within consumer discretionary.

**RDDT ($122.26, -3.93%)** — Reddit got slammed after a landmark youth social media addiction lawsuit spooked investors. Down 5% at the session lows before recovering slightly. This is a name with regulatory tail risk baked in now. I'd stay cautious until the legal picture clarifies.

**PINS ($17.96, -1.32%)** — Pinterest declining but not as badly as the social media peers. Lower exposure to the youth addiction lawsuit narrative than RDDT or SNAP. The stock has been quietly building a base around $17-18. Not actionable here but worth watching.

**DIS ($93.52, -1.30%)** — Disney holding relatively well. Parks revenue provides a floor that pure-play streamers don't have. The Iran situation could theoretically hurt international tourism but that's a second-order effect. Trading near the bottom of its range.

**UBER ($70.55, -6.07%)** — One of the day's worst performers in our universe. Ride-hailing is directly exposed to gas prices, and with WTI at $99.64 driver economics get crushed. The market is pricing in margin compression from fuel surcharges that won't fully offset cost increases. Sub-$70 is a critical level.

**SHOP ($111.22, -3.65%)** — Shopify tracking the broader e-commerce selloff. Higher energy costs feed through to shipping costs and consumer spending reduction — both headwinds for the platform model. The $105-110 support zone is the next test.

**SPOT ($473.94, -1.48%)** — Spotify showing relative resilience similar to Netflix. Subscription stickiness is a defensible moat in this environment. Content costs are mostly fixed so margin story intact regardless of energy prices.

**SNAP ($3.88, -3.24%)** — SNAP continues to bleed. The youth social media lawsuit is an existential overhang for a company already struggling with monetization. Sub-$4 is deeply distressed territory. I'm increasingly skeptical this company survives independently.

**ETSY ($47.32, -1.70%)** — Tracking the market. Etsy's handmade/unique value proposition should theoretically benefit from trade-down dynamics, but the stock hasn't caught a bid yet. Tariff exposure on international sellers is a real concern.

**W ($70.64, -3.97%)** — Wayfair continues to struggle. Big-ticket home furnishing is exactly what consumers cut when they're scared. Niraj Shah sold 1,571 shares this week — insider selling at the lows is never a great signal. Avoid.

**LYFT ($12.76, -2.60%)** — Same gas price problem as UBER but worse, because Lyft has less scale and thinner margins. This is an existential environment for the #2 ride-hail player if oil stays above $95.

**V ($295.14, -3.23%)** — Visa is a high-quality name that's getting caught in the growth-to-value rotation. Payment volumes will slow if consumer spending contracts, but the franchise is incredibly durable. This is a name you want to own on the other side of the geopolitical overhang.

**WSM ($186.52, -3.66%)** — Williams-Sonoma dragged down with the home/furnishings complex. Higher-end consumer is more insulated than Wayfair's customer, but sentiment is negative across the space.

**WMG ($22.85, -3.38%)** — Warner Music tracking broader entertainment weakness. Music streaming royalties provide some stability but label economics are getting squeezed by AI-generated content concerns and slower ad spending.

**MDB ($234.62, -2.76%)** — MongoDB continuing its slide from the $280+ levels earlier this month. Enterprise software spending is the next shoe to drop if the macro deteriorates further. The $230 support level needs to hold.

**CART ($45.50, -1.09%)** — Instacart showing interesting relative strength. Grocery delivery actually benefits from higher gas prices — consumers may prefer delivery over driving. One of the few names where the Iran situation could be a tailwind.

**DASH ($150.22, -3.85%)** — DoorDash hit a new 52-week low today at $143.30 before bouncing. Delivery economics get pressured by higher fuel costs, but DASH has more pricing power than LYFT/UBER because food delivery is less price-elastic than ride-hailing. Still, the trend is your enemy here.

**LYV ($145.71, -2.89%)** — Live Nation settling after the DOJ antitrust resolution. The consent decree (capping ticketing fees at 15%, extending by 8 years) removes the worst-case scenario but limits upside optionality. Live events demand remains robust — record 2025 results ($25.2B revenue, +9%) support the thesis.

**NON-CONSENSUS IDEAS — EVENING UPDATE**

**1. Off-Price Retail Long (TJX/BURL/ROST) — Thesis Strengthening**
Today confirmed the trade-down thesis. All three off-price names outperformed the S&P by 50-100bps on a day the index dropped 1.67%. With consumer sentiment at 2026 lows and gas prices approaching $4/gallon, the acceleration into value retail is just beginning. Energy inflation is the catalyst consensus is underweighting. Adding to positions on any pullback to their 20-day moving averages.

**2. Short Ride-Hailing (UBER/LYFT) vs. Long Grocery Delivery (CART) — New Setup**
Today crystallized an interesting pairs trade. UBER -6.07% and LYFT -2.60% vs. CART -1.09%. The divergence makes fundamental sense: higher gas prices crush ride-hailing driver economics but potentially drive more grocery delivery volume as consumers avoid driving. This spread widens if WTI holds above $95. Initiating a small position Monday.

**3. Energy Complex — Tail Risk Not Priced**
WTI at $99.64 and Brent at $112.57 reflect a partial Hormuz disruption. Full closure would send crude to $130-150. The options market is pricing ~25% probability of that outcome — I think it's closer to 35-40% based on the military posture escalation. XLE calls or OXY/CVX remain attractive as hedges. The 10-day deadline extension to April 6 is a countdown, not a resolution.

**4. Visa/Payments on the Pullback — Getting Interesting**
V at $295 (-3.23% today) is approaching levels that historically represent 3-5 year buying opportunities. Payment networks are secular winners regardless of economic cycles. If V touches $280, that's a conviction entry. The market is conflating cyclical spending softness with structural impairment — classic overreaction.

**5. Reddit Risk — Social Media Regulation Accelerating**
RDDT -3.93% on the youth addiction lawsuit is the canary in the coal mine for social media regulation. SNAP at $3.88 is already priced for distress. This regulatory wave has legs — bipartisan support, election year dynamics, and real legal liability. Stay away from SNAP and underweight RDDT until the legal framework clarifies. META's scale and diversification provide better insulation.

**WOODCOCK TREND SCOUT**

**Brand Breakdown:** Free People (1), ASOS/YAS (2), Urban Outfitters (3), ASOS/Miss Selfridge (2), Anthropologie (2), ASOS/Reclaimed Vintage (1)

- YAS Festival Crochet Lace Insert Boho Maxi Skirt — cream, crochet lace panels ($83-104)
- Elliatt Tilly Tiered Maxi Dress — tiered romantic silhouette ($335)
- Anthropologie Floral Tiered Maxi Dress — floral motif, tiered construction ($126-168)
- Kimchi Blue Stevie Crochet Bell Sleeve Flyaway Cardigan — cream, bell sleeves ($79)
- Out From Under Tia Crochet Beach Cover-Up — scoop neck, beach/resort ($69)

**Juniors Market (4 items):**
- Kimchi Blue Tulum Crochet Halter Top — cropped, scalloped v-neck ($4.99-39)
- Miss Selfridge Crochet Tassel Boho Jumper — cream, tassel detailing ($59.99)
- YAS Festival Crochet Trim Cami Midi Dress — cream, cami neckline ($65-85)
- Miss Selfridge Crochet Tie Front Boho Cardigan — cream, tie closure ($49-65)

**Girls Market (1 item):**

**Key Trend Observations:** Crochet is THE dominant fabrication across all markets — 10 of 11 finds feature crochet construction or crochet trim detailing. The color story is overwhelmingly cream/coconut milk (neutral palette). Boho/festival aesthetic is driving the silhouettes. Price architecture spans aggressively from $4.99 (clearance entry) to $335 (aspirational Anthropologie). The tiered maxi dress is emerging as a key silhouette alongside the crochet top. This is a clear signal for spring/summer 2026 — crochet is not a micro-trend, it's the season's macro story.

**OVERNIGHT POSITIONING**

Watch Sunday night futures closely — the Iran deadline extension to April 6 means the next 10 days are a pressure cooker. Asian markets will react to any weekend developments on Hormuz shipping. Japan's Nikkei and Hong Kong's Hang Seng are the most exposed given energy import dependence. Europe opens Monday with the EU-US trade deal advancement as a modest positive offset.

Key overnight data: China PMI (Saturday/Sunday release) could move Asia significantly — Bloomberg's reporting on China edging toward reflation suggests upside surprise risk. If China PMI beats, it's bullish for commodities but adds to the inflation narrative.

Risk positioning: I'd carry some energy longs (XLE, OXY) as geopolitical hedges over the weekend. Trim any overweight in ride-hailing (UBER/LYFT). The off-price retail trade (TJX/BURL/ROST) is a hold. Cash levels should be elevated — 15-20% minimum in this tape.

**ACTION ITEMS**

1. **Pre-market Monday:** Watch for any Iran/Hormuz developments over the weekend. Set alerts for WTI crude — a break above $100 will trigger another wave of selling in consumer discretionary.
2. **Monday AM:** Evaluate the UBER short / CART long pairs trade entry. Look for UBER below $69 and CART above $45 for optimal entry.
3. **China PMI release (weekend):** If above consensus, consider adding to commodity-exposed longs Monday AM. If miss, the deflation narrative returns and tech could catch a relief bid.
4. **April 6 Iran deadline:** Mark your calendar. This is the next binary event. Build optionality (long straddles on XLE or SPY) into the event.
5. **Portfolio review:** With the S&P down five straight weeks, review stop-loss levels across all positions. This is not the tape for heroes — protect capital and wait for clarity.

title: Evening Briefing — Monday, March 30, 2026
content:
**Good Evening, Jazzy — Monday, March 30, 2026**

Oil breached $100 and stayed there. The Strait of Hormuz crisis is now the single dominant variable in global markets, and today's session showed the Dow clinging to green on defensive rotation while tech and growth got smoked. Powell tried to calm nerves on inflation but futures are already sliding — tonight is about positioning for a week loaded with jobs data and a Middle East situation that's only escalating.

**TODAY'S MARKET WRAP**

**US Markets (Close):**
S&P 500: 6,343.84 (-25.01, -0.39%) | Nasdaq Composite: 20,794.64 (-153.72, -0.73%) | Dow Jones: 45,216.66 (+50.02, +0.11%) | Russell 2000: ~2,449.70 (~-1.75%) | VIX: 30.61 (-0.44, -1.42%). The Dow barely escaped red in a last-minute bid into blue-chip defensives, while the Nasdaq extended its correction — now down 10% from recent highs. The Russell got hit hardest as small-cap growth names bore the brunt of the risk-off rotation. Volume ran above average across all major indices, with AMZN seeing 56M shares traded vs. a 44M average. The VIX remains above 30, a level that historically precedes either capitulation or further downside — not a comfortable perch.

**What Moved:** The Iran conflict and $100+ oil remain the gravitational center of all price action. Crude's surge to $101+ WTI triggered a violent sector rotation — Energy surged (XLE +22% YTD, the "surprise trade of 2026"), while Technology (-1%+), Consumer Discretionary (-12.3% MTD), and Semiconductors (SMH -3.12%) got crushed. The VanEck Semiconductor ETF saw aggressive distribution as investors questioned peak AI demand narratives amid a real-world energy crisis. Exxon and Chevron led the Dow higher, masking broad-based weakness. Palo Alto Networks (PANW +5.11%) was a rare bright spot after the CEO dropped $10M on open-market purchases. Morgan Stanley cut META's target to $775 from $825 but maintained it as a top pick. Argan (AGX +37.9%) and Unity (U +13.5%) were among the day's biggest movers.

**Treasuries & Rates:** 10-Year: 4.35% (down ~10bps from Friday's 4.42%) | 2-Year: ~3.96% (last confirmed 3/26). The curve steepened modestly with the 10Y/2Y spread at ~0.39%. Yields dropped after Powell's comments that tariff-related inflation was a one-time price bump and that the Fed has limited control over supply-driven oil shocks. Fed's Miran still backs cuts, saying rates could be "about a point" lower in 2026. However, three Fed officials expressed growing anxiety — Fed's Cook said inflation is now more at risk than jobs due to the war. The bond market is caught between flight-to-safety buying and inflation fears from $100+ oil. This tension won't resolve until either oil breaks back below $90 or above $120.

**Oil & Commodities:** WTI Crude: $101.17 (+$7.37, +7.86%) | Brent Crude: $112.57 (+4.22%). Oil shattered the $100 level and held — first time since the early days of the Ukraine invasion in 2022. The Strait of Hormuz blockade has interrupted ~20% of global oil supply, embedding a $14-18/bbl war premium in Brent. Goldman hasn't ruled out $130-150 if the blockade persists. Late in the session, reports surfaced that Trump told aides he's willing to end the military campaign even if Hormuz remains closed — futures trimmed gains slightly. Gold: $4,493.12 (+$111.21, +2.54%) — safe-haven demand relentless. Silver: $69.74 (+2.43%). Natural Gas: $3.025/MMBtu, trading 20% below EIA's 2026 forecast but European TTF has surged 34% since March 1 on LNG supply route concerns.

**After-Hours Movers:** S&P 500 futures -0.18%, Nasdaq 100 futures -0.42%, Dow futures -0.15%. Bloomberg reported Iran struck a Kuwaiti oil tanker in Dubai waters, which pushed oil higher in extended trading. Trump threatened to destroy Iran's oil wells and Kharg Island without a deal to immediately reopen Hormuz. Nike (NKE) reports Tuesday after the bell — the week's marquee earnings event. 77 companies reported today, 126 scheduled for Tuesday.

**Geopolitical Update:** The Iran war enters its fifth week with no de-escalation in sight. The Strait of Hormuz crossing volume has dropped 70%+ from pre-conflict levels. Trump is oscillating between escalation rhetoric (threatening to destroy Iran's oil infrastructure) and potential off-ramps (reportedly willing to accept Hormuz closure). Iran struck a Kuwaiti oil tanker in Dubai waters late today, widening the conflict's geographic footprint. Russia welcomed an oil tanker in Cuba after Trump softened the U.S. blockade approach. Europe faces a second energy crisis — Dutch TTF gas nearly doubled to over EUR60/MWh. The ECB warned of stagflation risk in Germany and Italy. An FT investigation found $580M in bets on falling oil prices placed 15 minutes before Trump's March 23 statement on postponing attacks — insider trading speculation is building. G7 emergency meetings are reportedly failing to produce results. The market implications are clear: this is not a one-week event. Position for sustained elevated oil and persistent volatility.

**STOCK BRIEFING — END OF DAY**

**META ($536.38, -7.89%)** — Ugly session. Morgan Stanley cut its target to $775 from $825 but maintained META as a top pick. The stock gapped down from a $582 open to close near its lows at $536. The broader tech sell-off plus AI anxiety headwinds (Axios flagged AI disruption fears as one of only two market catalysts alongside the Iran war) are creating a toxic cocktail. META is now trading well below its 200-day moving average. I still think this is a name you want to own on the other side of this, but the tape is not your friend right now.

**GOOGL ($275.50, -0.64%)** — Traded in a narrow $274-$279 range, closing near the bottom. Relatively defensive for a mega-cap tech name today, but still down meaningfully from its March high of $312. The ad business provides more recession insulation than pure-play tech peers, but a prolonged oil shock that tips the economy will eventually hit ad budgets. Hold.

**AMZN ($200.33, -3.01%)** — Down $58 from its peak and now firmly in correction territory. Volume ran hot at 56M shares vs. 44M average — this is institutional distribution, not retail panic. The e-commerce giant faces a double whammy: consumer spending headwinds from $5+ gas prices and AWS's energy cost exposure. At $200, the stock is pricing in a meaningful growth slowdown.

**NFLX ($92.74, -1.95%)** — Continued its slide, trading near the day's low. Netflix has dropped from $134 to $92 in recent months. Consumer discretionary spending on streaming subscriptions becomes vulnerable when gas prices eat into household budgets. The content moat is real but the multiple compression has been severe.

**TJX ($157.57, -0.12%)** — The off-price king barely budged in a volatile session — exactly what you want from a defensive retail name. TJX just hit $60B in revenue and is the anti-consumer-spending-collapse trade. When consumers trade down, they trade into TJX. This is a core hold.

**BURL ($312.06, -0.22%)** — Similarly resilient. Short interest increased 24.8%, which tells me bears are starting to question whether the off-price trade is overextended. I disagree — BURL benefits directly from the trade-down effect. Burlington's value positioning is actually strengthened by the macro backdrop.

**ROST ($148.20, -0.35%)** — Completed the off-price trifecta of relative strength today. All three off-price retailers (TJX, BURL, ROST) outperformed the broad market by a wide margin. This sector is the consumer playbook in a stagflationary environment.

**RDDT ($124.13, -4.70%)** — Reddit's implosion continues. The stock has been roughly halved from its highs near $260. The Invezz piece asking "buy the dip or sell the rip?" captures the debate. User engagement metrics remain strong but the company's ad-dependent revenue model is vulnerable in a downturn. Too early to catch this knife.

**PINS ($18.01, +1.41%)** — Rare green day. Pinterest quietly outperformed the broader tech space. The stock has been beaten down enough that even modest positive sentiment generates bounces. The visual discovery platform has e-commerce tailwinds but needs to prove it can monetize them at scale.

**DIS ($94.12, +1.80%)** — Disney was a bright spot — up on optimism around a new Disneyland Paris attraction and soaring streaming revenue reports. The parks business provides real-asset backing that tech doesn't have. DIS is one of the better defensive-growth positions in this environment.

**UBER ($69.67, -0.45%)** — Grinding lower. Down 11.5% YTD and well below its 52-week high of $102. Rising fuel costs directly pressure driver economics and Uber's take rate. The rideshare model is structurally challenged when gas is at $5+. I'd want to see oil break before adding here.

**SHOP ($111.85, -1.46%)** — Shopify traded in a $110-$115 range. The e-commerce enabler is caught between secular growth tailwinds (more merchants going online) and cyclical headwinds (consumer spending pullback). The stock is reasonably valued at current levels but needs macro cooperation to work.

**SPOT ($475.00, +0.53%)** — Spotify was one of the few tech names to close green. The subscription model provides more revenue visibility than ad-dependent peers. Music streaming is also more recession-resistant than other discretionary spending. Steady as she goes.

**SNAP ($4.10, +4.33%)** — Up on the day but context matters — SNAP is down 51% in 2026 and the EU probe overhang remains. At $4.10 this is a sub-$7B market cap company fighting for relevance. The bounce feels like short covering, not conviction.

**ETSY ($48.35, +2.19%)** — Modest bounce from recent weakness. Etsy's marketplace model has some defensibility in a downturn (handmade/unique goods don't compete directly with mass retail) but the stock has been weak. Needs to prove Q1 numbers can stabilize buyer counts.

**W ($70.65, -1.19%)** — Wayfair continues to bleed. Home furnishing e-commerce is about as cyclically exposed as it gets right now — housing market weakness plus consumer pullback is a double headwind. No reason to be here.

**LYFT ($12.65, -2.31%)** — Same fuel-cost headwinds as UBER but with a weaker balance sheet and no diversification beyond rideshare. Volume was anemic at 3.2M vs. 21.7M average, suggesting capitulation-level disinterest. This is a show-me story at best.

**V ($299.00, +1.18%)** — Visa held up well and crossed back toward $300. Payment networks are the ultimate toll-road businesses — they benefit from inflation (higher nominal spending) while bearing zero credit risk. V is a core defensive holding in this environment.

**WSM ($178.50, -0.83%)** — Williams-Sonoma continues to drift lower. The premium home goods retailer is caught in the housing/consumer spending vortex. Down from its recent highs but the brand portfolio (Pottery Barn, West Elm) has pricing power. Watch for signs of stabilization.

**WMG ($23.88, -0.50%)** — Warner Music is a quiet compounder. Music rights and streaming royalties provide recurring revenue that's largely uncorrelated with macro cycles. At $24, the valuation isn't demanding. This is a hold-and-collect name.

**MDB ($245.00, -1.60%)** — MongoDB drifted lower with the broader tech sell-off. The database company's growth story is intact but multiple compression in the tech sector is hitting everything. Down from $253 last week. The AI infrastructure narrative provides a floor, but sentiment is challenging.

**CART ($37.44, -1.57%)** — Instacart was upgraded to Buy from Hold at Jefferies today with a $45 target (up from $38). The grocery delivery platform has defensibility — people still need to eat — but the stock didn't react much to the upgrade, suggesting the market wants to see execution first.

**DASH ($158.80, -2.32%)** — DoorDash traded in a $156-$165 range, closing near the lower end. Food delivery has some recession resilience but rising fuel costs pressure unit economics on the driver side. The stock is holding up better than most growth names but isn't immune.

**LYV ($147.50, +1.23%)** — Live Nation was part of today's Wall Street analyst call roundup from 24/7 Wall St. Live events continue to see strong demand — the experience economy is proving more resilient than goods spending. A decent defensive-growth option in the consumer space.

**NON-CONSENSUS IDEAS — EVENING UPDATE**

**1. Long Off-Price Retail (TJX/BURL/ROST) vs. Short Premium Discretionary**
Today confirmed the thesis. TJX, BURL, and ROST all posted near-flat returns while the consumer discretionary sector got demolished (-12.3% MTD). The trade-down effect is accelerating as gas prices eat into household budgets. BURL's rising short interest (up 24.8%) suggests bears are positioning against this trade — they're wrong. The off-price model gets stronger in exactly this environment. Stay long.

**2. Energy Has Further to Run — Don't Fight the Strait**
Energy is up 22% YTD and today's action shows no signs of exhaustion. Goldman hasn't ruled out $130-150 Brent. The Strait of Hormuz blockade has removed 20% of global supply and Iran's strike on a Kuwaiti tanker tonight is escalatory. The consensus "oil will come back down" call has been dead wrong for five weeks. Stay overweight energy. If you're not positioned, XLE or direct exposure via XOM/CVX is still the play.

**3. Visa (V) as the Inflation Hedge Nobody's Talking About**
V closed at $299, up 1.18% while most of the market bled. Here's the non-consensus angle: Visa actually benefits from inflation. Higher nominal consumer spending = higher payment volumes = higher revenue. Zero credit risk. Zero inventory. Zero fuel cost exposure. In a stagflationary environment where both growth and value are challenged, payment networks are the cleanest positioning. The stock is well below its 52-week high of $375, offering entry.

**4. Fade the AI Anxiety Narrative — Selectively**
Axios nailed it: the market has only two catalysts — AI anxiety and Trump/Iran. The AI fear got compounded today by the broader tech sell-off, but the fundamental demand for AI infrastructure hasn't changed. When the geopolitical fog lifts (and it will), the AI names that got caught in the crossfire will rip. SPOT and DIS showed today that quality names with diversified revenue can hold. Build watchlists now for META, GOOGL, and MDB for when the turn comes.

**5. Short SNAP Below $4 — The Spiral Accelerates**
SNAP bounced 4.3% today but is down 51% YTD. The EU probe creates additional regulatory overhead that a sub-$7B company simply cannot absorb while competing against META and TikTok. Every bounce is a selling opportunity. The company faces an existential question about whether it can sustain itself as a standalone entity. If you're positioned short, today's bounce is a gift.

**WOODCOCK TREND SCOUT**

Last Run Date: March 27, 2026 | Total Finds: 11 items | Primary Trend: Crochet & Boho Revival

Brand Breakdown: Free People (1), ASOS/YAS/Miss Selfridge (4), Urban Outfitters/Kimchi Blue/Out From Under (3), Anthropologie/Elliatt (2), Reclaimed Vintage (1)

**Missy Market (6 items):** Free People Lily Crochet Top ($75-78), Elliatt Tilly Tiered Maxi Dress ($335), Floral Tiered Maxi Dress ($126-168) from Anthropologie, Kimchi Blue Stevie Crochet Bell Sleeve Cardigan ($79), Out From Under Tia Crochet Beach Cover-Up ($69), YAS Festival Crochet Lace Insert Boho Maxi Skirt ($83-104).

**Juniors Market (5 items):** Kimchi Blue Tulum Crochet Halter Top ($4.99-39), Miss Selfridge Crochet Tassel Boho Jumper ($59.99), YAS Festival Boho Crochet Trim Cami Midi Dress ($65-85), Miss Selfridge Crochet Tie Front Boho Cardigan ($49-65), Reclaimed Vintage Festival Crochet Mini Dress ($75-95).

**Girls Market:** No items this cycle.

**Key Trend Observations:** Crochet is the dominant fabrication story across both Missy and Juniors — appearing in 9 of 11 items. The boho/festival aesthetic is driving silhouette choices (maxis, halters, cardigans, cover-ups). Color palette is overwhelmingly cream/white/neutral. Price architecture ranges from aggressive ($4.99 markdown at UO) to aspirational ($335 at Anthropologie), suggesting the trend has mass-market legs. The tiered maxi dress continues to gain momentum at the Missy level. This is a clear signal for Spring/Summer '26 production planning — crochet, boho, cream tones, and tiered construction should be front and center.

**OVERNIGHT POSITIONING**

Asian session will react to the Iran-Kuwait tanker strike — expect Nikkei and Hang Seng to open weak. European markets face the dual headwind of TTF gas prices (near-doubled) and ECB stagflation warnings. Key overnight data: Japan Industrial Production (expected weak), China PMI (Tuesday morning — critical for global growth read). Oil will remain the overnight variable — any Hormuz headline can move markets 1-2% in either direction. Position defensively: overweight energy and payment networks (V), underweight unprofitable tech and consumer discretionary. Keep powder dry for potential capitulation entries in quality growth names if VIX spikes above 35.

**ACTION ITEMS**

1. **Watch Tuesday's JOLTS report** — Job openings data will set the tone for the week's labor market narrative. Weakness here accelerates the flight-to-safety trade.
2. **Nike (NKE) earnings Tuesday after the bell** — Critical read on global consumer health. Guidance on China and Europe will be market-moving given the macro backdrop.
3. **Monitor Strait of Hormuz headlines overnight** — The Kuwaiti tanker strike is escalatory. Any further widening of the conflict's geographic scope could push Brent toward $120.
4. **Pre-market Wednesday: ADP Employment** — Another labor data point ahead of Friday's NFP (which lands on Good Friday when markets are closed, compressing the reaction window).
5. **Review off-price retail positioning** — BURL's rising short interest creates potential squeeze dynamics. Consider adding to TJX/BURL/ROST on any dip this week.

title: Evening Briefing — Tuesday, March 31, 2026
content:
**Good Evening, Jazzy — Tuesday, March 31, 2026**

Iran peace signals lit the fuse on the biggest rally since May, with the Nasdaq ripping nearly 4% as the crowd scrambled to cover shorts into quarter-end. But Brent still closed at $118 and Hormuz remains shut — tonight is about whether Tehran's ceasefire signals survive contact with reality, and whether this was a genuine inflection or a vicious bear market squeeze heading into April.

**TODAY'S MARKET WRAP**

**US Markets (Close):** S&P 500 6,528.52 (+190.71, +2.91%) | Nasdaq 21,590.63 (+809.53, +3.83%) | Dow 46,341.51 (+1,125.37, +2.49%) | Russell 2000 2,496.37 (+82.37, +3.41%) | VIX 25.25 (-5.35, -17.51%). Best day since May across all major indices. 441 of 500 S&P names closed green. 10 of 11 sectors higher — Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services led, Utilities sole decliner. Biotech (XBI) surged 7.4%, Cannabis (MSOS) ripped 13.1%. Energy (XLE) -1.85% as oil pulled back on peace hopes. Volume elevated on quarter-end rebalancing; Marvell alone traded 50.9M shares (194% above 3-month avg) after Nvidia's $2B investment announcement. Despite today's surge, Q1 2026 closes as the worst quarter for stocks in nearly four years.

**What Moved:** The primary catalyst was WSJ reporting Trump told aides he's willing to end the Iran war even without reopening Hormuz immediately, combined with Iranian President Pezeshkian signaling readiness for a ceasefire under security guarantees. This triggered a massive short squeeze across growth and beta. Consumer Confidence surprised to the upside at 91.8 vs 87.9 consensus (Present Situation +4.6 pts), giving bulls additional ammo. Nvidia's $2B stake in Marvell kept the AI trade alive. Apellis Pharma (APLS) exploded +136% on clinical data; Centessa (CNTA) +45%.

**Treasuries & Rates:** 10Y yield fell to 4.22% from 4.31% morning high as rate cut optimism grew on the risk-on rotation. 30Y at 4.89% (-3bps). The curve bull-steepened modestly. Powell (speaking to Harvard students) said the Fed would be inclined to hold steady and look past the energy price shock, but cautioned the Fed might hike if elevated energy prices start boosting consumer inflation expectations — a subtle but important hawkish lean that the market largely ignored today. Gov. Barr spoke on stablecoins; Vice Chair Bowman on small business credit access. Mortgage rates at 6.36% (30Y fixed), down 11bps.

**Oil & Commodities:** WTI settled $101.38 (-1.46%) as peace optimism took the froth off, but Brent May contract surged to $118.35 (+5%) — the dislocation between WTI and Brent reflects the physical supply disruption from Hormuz closure. Brent posted its best monthly gain since 1988 (+60% in March). Iran attacked a Kuwaiti oil tanker off the Dubai coast today, a reminder that the shooting hasn't actually stopped. U.S. gasoline now averaging over $4/gallon nationally. Gold holding elevated levels near $5,400. Nat gas soft on warmer forecasts.

**After-Hours Movers:** Nike (NKE) reporting Q4 after the bell — key read on consumer health and tariff pass-through. RH reported Q4/FY2025 results. McCormick (MKC) beat and is in talks with Unilever on a potential deal. Sysco nearing $29B acquisition of Restaurant Depot (WSJ).

**Geopolitical Update:** The day's dominant story was multi-sourced reporting that Trump is willing to end the Iran conflict without requiring Hormuz reopening as a precondition. Defense Secretary Hegseth called the coming days "decisive." Iranian President Pezeshkian said Iran is open to ceasefire under security guarantees — first significant de-escalation signal from Tehran. However, Trump simultaneously renewed threats to destroy Iran's water and energy infrastructure, keeping the outcome binary. US-UK relations deteriorating over Iran policy coordination (FT). The Strait of Hormuz remains closed. This is NOT a done deal — it's a negotiating posture from both sides, and the tanker attack off Dubai today shows hostilities continue. But the market is pricing in progress, and positioning was extremely short.

**STOCK BRIEFING — END OF DAY**

**META ($572.13, ~+3.8%)** — Rode the broad Nasdaq rip higher. Iran de-escalation reduces the tail risk on ad budgets that had been compressing. Reels monetization trajectory intact. Still the cleanest large-cap ad play but watch for Q2 guidance sensitivity to tariff uncertainty on DTC advertisers.

**GOOGL ($276.20, ~+3.5%)** — Benefited from the broad tech rally and risk-on rotation. Search remains defensible; Cloud growth is the swing factor. AI narrative supportive but regulatory overhang persists. Solid quarter-end positioning trade.

**AMZN ($209.19, +4.10%)** — Led the mega-caps on the day. AWS re-acceleration narrative intact, and the consumer side benefits disproportionately from any tariff/geopolitical normalization. $4 gasoline is a headwind for delivery economics but the Street is looking through it.

**NFLX ($96.15, ~+3.5%)** — Strong day in sympathy with broader consumer discretionary. Ad-tier growth continues to be the story. Relatively insulated from tariff/trade disruption compared to physical goods names. Valuation stretched but momentum buyers in control.

**TJX ($157.57, ~+2.5%)** — Off-price retail rallied on the risk-on move. TJX is the best-positioned off-pricer in a tariff world — their buying model actually benefits from brand inventory dislocation. Watch for any guidance commentary on freight cost pass-through with $4 gas.

**BURL ($329.27, ~+3.0%)** — Burlington rallied in sympathy with off-price peers. Incoming inventory from Woodcock and other vendors should hit stores in Q2. The 10 Burlington POs canceling today (from the morning brief) remain a near-term headache but the long-term category growth story is intact.

**ROST ($211.69, ~+2.5%)** — Participated in the off-price rally. Ross continues to execute on inventory management. Tariff disruption benefits the treasure-hunt model. Less upside optionality than TJX but steadier execution.

**RDDT ($124.13, +5.4%)** — Reddit ripped on the risk-on day as heavily shorted names squeezed. Down 45.6% YTD and 51.4% below 52-week high of $270.71. Advertising revenue growth is the key variable; user engagement trends still solid. This is a sentiment and positioning story right now.

**PINS ($17.76, ~+4.0%)** — Bounced but faces serious headwinds. Class action lawsuits filed today over tariff-related revenue warnings. Elliott's $1B investment and $3.5B buyback authorization provide a floor, but the legal overhang and ad spending slowdown are real. Tread carefully.

**DIS ($96.38, ~+3.0%)** — Disney rallied with broader market. Streaming profitability improving. Parks business would benefit from any geopolitical normalization that helps consumer confidence. Theme park international exposure a wildcard if travel patterns shift.

**UBER ($71.97, ~+3.5%)** — Solid rally. $4 gasoline is a mixed bag — increases consumer demand for rideshare vs driving but pressures driver economics. The gas dynamic creates short-term noise but network effects are strengthening. Watch for any driver supply commentary.

**SHOP ($118.58, ~+4.5%)** — Shopify benefited from the growth rotation. Platform model means tariff headwinds flow through merchants, not directly to SHOP. Enterprise adoption continues. Valuation demands sustained GMV acceleration.

**SPOT ($484.91, ~+3.5%)** — Spotify continued its strong run. Price hike pass-through boosting ARPU. Podcast monetization inflecting. One of the cleaner growth stories in the portfolio — insulated from tariff/geopolitical noise.

**SNAP ($4.10, +13%+)** — The big mover in the portfolio today. Activist Irenic Capital took a 2.5% stake and sent a letter demanding 21% workforce reduction, shutdown of Specs AR business, and cost rationalization. Irenic's price target: $26.37 vs today's ~$4.10. This is a real catalyst — Irenic has a track record. Watch for management response.

**ETSY ($49.25, ~+3.5%)** — Rallied with broader market. Tariff disruption could actually benefit Etsy's domestic artisan model. Marketplace economics improving. Still needs to demonstrate sustained buyer growth.

**W ($69.50, ~+5.0%)** — Wayfair bounced hard on the risk-on day. Housing market sensitivity means rate cut expectations are a major driver. $4 gasoline and consumer confidence matter for big-ticket furniture purchases. High beta play on any macro improvement.

**LYFT ($12.65, ~+4.0%)** — Followed Uber higher. Same gas price dynamics apply. Market share stabilizing. The valuation gap to Uber is extreme, which creates optionality but also reflects structural disadvantages.

**V ($302.24, +0.90%)** — Visa underperformed the broader market as a low-beta name. Cross-border transaction volumes benefit from travel normalization. Consumer spending data flowing through the network remains healthy. Defensive positioning in a risk-on day explains the relative lag.

**WSM ($181.92, ~+3.0%)** — Williams-Sonoma rallied with consumer discretionary. Premium home furnishing benefits from any housing recovery. Q4 holiday season setup will be key. Solid execution but valuation leaves limited margin of error.

**WMG ($24.10, ~+2.0%)** — Warner Music underperformed slightly. Music streaming economics improving but WMG trades as a yield/defensive name. Catalog value is the long-term thesis. Limited upside in a risk-on tape.

**MDB ($233.61, ~+4.5%)** — MongoDB rallied with growth/software. AI workload tailwinds are real and accelerating. Enterprise adoption broadening. Valuation requires continued 25%+ revenue growth.

**CART ($37.19, +4.12%)** — Instacart had a solid day. Grocery delivery demand steady. $4 gasoline actually helps the use case — consumers more willing to pay for delivery when driving costs spike. Enterprise platform (Caper Carts, ads) diversifying the revenue base.

**DASH ($148.01, ~+4.0%)** — DoorDash rallied on the broad risk-on move. Launched gas relief program for Dashers (10% cash back + weekly $5-$15 relief) — smart move to retain driver supply amid $4 gas. New Foot Locker retail partnership covering ~1,300 locations shows continued non-restaurant expansion.

**LYV ($148.69, ~+3.0%)** — Live Nation bounced. Live events demand remains resilient. Any geopolitical normalization helps international touring economics. Valuation compressed on regulatory concerns but the demand side is strong.

**NON-CONSENSUS IDEAS — EVENING UPDATE**

**1. Iran De-Escalation Is a Positioning Unwind, Not a Fundamental Shift (Yet)**
Today's 3-4% rip was overwhelmingly driven by short covering and quarter-end window dressing, not genuine fundamental re-rating. Hormuz is STILL closed. Iran attacked a tanker TODAY. The ceasefire signals are real but both sides are still shooting. If you were short into this, the pain was real — but chasing the long side here at 6,528 with VIX still at 25 and Brent at $118 is paying full price for an outcome that's maybe 40% probability. I'd be selling into strength on the index level and getting more surgical in single names.

**2. SNAP Activist Play Has Real Legs**
Irenic at $4.10 targeting $26.37 is a 6.4x upside target. The playbook they're proposing — cut headcount 21%, kill Specs, rationalize costs — is exactly what Snap needs. Management has been empire-building while the stock has cratered. The key question is whether Evan Spiegel will engage or stonewall. His super-voting shares make a hostile approach impossible, so this is a persuasion game. I'd start building a position here — risk/reward is asymmetric if management listens, and the floor is established by Irenic's accumulation.

**3. Off-Price Retail Is the Tariff Winner Nobody's Pricing**
TJX, BURL, and ROST all rallied today but the real story is structural: tariff disruption creates MORE inventory dislocation, which FEEDS the off-price buying model. When brands can't sell through at full price because of tariff-driven price increases, off-price channels absorb that inventory at steeper discounts. This is the one sector where tariff chaos is genuinely accretive. TJX is the cleanest way to play it.

**4. Energy Names Lagging Despite $100+ Oil Is Telling**
XLE was the second-worst sector today (-1.85%) despite WTI at $101. The market is pricing in that if peace happens, oil crashes. If you believe the war ends soon, energy shorts make sense. But if you think the ceasefire talk is premature (which I do), energy names at these levels with $100+ oil are printing cash and the market is giving you free optionality. Watch the front-month Brent/WTI spread — if it stays wide, the physical market is telling you Hormuz isn't opening soon.

**5. Pinterest's Legal Overhang Creates a Tactical Entry**
Class action lawsuits are typically noise, not signal — they get filed after any significant stock decline. The real story at PINS is Elliott's $1B position and $3.5B buyback authorization. At $17.76, you're buying below where a sophisticated activist is accumulating. The ad revenue tariff headwind is real but temporary. If Iran de-escalation progresses, ad budgets recover and PINS has significant operating leverage.

**WOODCOCK TREND SCOUT**

Trend data unavailable — the Woodcock trend API did not return data on this evening's pull. Will retry on the next scheduled run. If the system is down, Jazzy should check the dashboard directly at localhost:3456.

**OVERNIGHT POSITIONING**

Asian markets will react to the Iran peace signals — expect Nikkei and Hang Seng to gap up following the US session. Watch for any overnight news from Tehran or Washington that could reverse or accelerate the ceasefire narrative. European open will be critical: Brent crude trading in London will be the real-time barometer of whether the market believes the de-escalation. Key overnight data: China PMI (manufacturing and non-manufacturing) drops overnight and will set the tone for Asia. If China data disappoints, it could cap the global rally. US futures will be sensitive to any Trump tweets on Iran — he has a pattern of making market-moving statements after midnight. Position size should reflect the binary nature of the geopolitical catalyst: if ceasefire talks collapse, today's gains reverse fast. Keep powder dry for that scenario.

**ACTION ITEMS**

1. **Watch Nike (NKE) after-hours** — Reporting Q4 tonight. Consumer health read, tariff pass-through commentary, and China guidance are the key items. Sets tone for consumer discretionary positioning.

2. **Monitor overnight Iran developments** — Any statement from Pezeshkian, Trump, or Hegseth could move futures 1-2% either direction. Set alerts for "Iran ceasefire" and "Hormuz."

3. **Pre-market Wednesday: ISM Manufacturing** — Consensus looking for contraction territory. A miss here would challenge today's risk-on narrative hard. Be ready to fade the rally if ISM disappoints.

4. **Review SNAP position sizing** — Irenic activist engagement is a legitimate catalyst. Consider starting a position at $4-4.10 range with a stop below $3.50. Risk/reward is compelling.

5. **Q2 sector allocation** — Today closed Q1. Worst quarter in four years. April historically strong. But the macro setup (Hormuz closed, $100+ oil, tariff uncertainty, Fed on hold) argues for quality over beta. Re-evaluate the growth vs. value tilt before Wednesday's open.

title: Evening Briefing — Wednesday, April 1, 2026
content:
**Good Evening, Jazzy — Wednesday, April 1, 2026**

Q2 opens with a relief rally as ceasefire chatter sends oil below $100 and lifts risk appetite across the board — but Iran's denial, Trump's primetime address tonight, and a VIX still north of 24 tell you this isn't over. Watch the 9 PM address closely; positioning into tomorrow hinges on whether Trump escalates or de-escalates.

**TODAY'S MARKET WRAP**

**US Markets (Close):**
- **S&P 500:** 6,575.32 (+46.80, +0.72%)
- **Nasdaq Composite:** 21,840.95 (+250.32, +1.16%)
- **Dow Jones Industrial Average:** 46,565.74 (+224.23, +0.48%)
- **Russell 2000:** 2,512.37 (+15.99, +0.64%)
- **VIX:** 24.54 (-0.71, -2.81%)

Tech led for a second consecutive session. Semiconductors ripped — Micron +8.96%, Intel +8.78% on its $14.2B Fab 34 buyback from Apollo. Energy was the clear loser, with the S&P 500 Energy Sector down over 4% as crude retreated on ceasefire hopes. Industrials and consumer discretionary posted solid gains. Airlines rallied (AAL +3.63%, DAL +2.17%). Nike was the day's biggest blue-chip casualty, cratering 15.52% on weak guidance and triple analyst downgrades. Volume ran above average on the first trading day of Q2.

**What Moved:** The dominant catalyst was Trump's Truth Social post claiming Iran's president requested a ceasefire — Iran denied it, but markets didn't care. The "Hormuz Hope" trade sent risk assets higher and crude lower. Retail sales came in better than expected (+0.6% vs +0.5% est.), supporting the consumer resilience narrative. SpaceX's confidential IPO filing added a speculative growth bid. Asia led the overnight session with South Korea's Kospi surging 8.44% and Japan's Nikkei up 5.24%. The OpenAI funding round with backing from Nvidia, Amazon, and SoftBank reinforced AI momentum.

**Treasuries & Rates:** The 10-year yield eased to 4.30%, down ~2bps, extending its retreat from the 8-month high of 4.44% hit two sessions ago. The 2-year settled at 3.81%. The 30-year came in around 4.90%. The 2s/10s spread sits at +49bps — positively sloped and reflecting growth uncertainty more than inflation fear at this point. Fed rate hike probability for 2026 dropped to ~20% from 35% last week after Powell's comments that tariffs produced a one-time price bump and the Fed has limited control over supply shocks like the oil surge. The bond market is telling you it's more worried about recession than runaway inflation.

**Oil & Commodities:** WTI crude settled around $99.96/bbl (-1.40%), dipping below $100 for the first time since mid-March. Brent crude at $101.89/bbl (-2.0%). The retreat is entirely ceasefire-driven — if Trump's address tonight disappoints, expect crude to snap back above $105 overnight. Gold surged to $4,784.60 (+2.27%), catching a bid from both the dollar pullback and falling real yields. Silver volatile around $75/oz. The gold move is notable — it's bouncing hard off the bear market correction (down 21% from the January $5,595 record) and JPMorgan's $6,300 year-end target gives it room.

**After-Hours Movers:** Eli Lilly (LLY) was up ~$40 after FDA approved its GLP-1 pill for weight loss. Oracle (ORCL) gained ~$3.30 on plans to cut thousands of jobs to redirect cash toward AI data center infrastructure — Barclays noted ORCL could triple revenue over 3 years with a smaller headcount. Dave & Buster's (PLAY) up 7% after guiding for higher same-store sales, revenue, and EBITDA in 2026. RH plunged 18% after guiding full-year revenue growth of 4-8% vs. Street's 8.8%.

**Geopolitical Update:** This is the most consequential night of the Iran conflict so far. Trump claims Iran's President Pezeshkian requested a ceasefire; Iran's foreign ministry called it "false and baseless." The U.S. precondition is the Strait of Hormuz being "open, free, and clear" — Iran's 5-point counterproposal insists on sovereignty over the strait. Trump addresses the nation at 9 PM ET — his first major national address on the conflict since the Feb. 28 joint US-Israeli strikes. Simultaneously, Pezeshkian will deliver his own "important message" to the American people. The death toll has exceeded 3,000 across the Middle East. In a separate bombshell, Trump told Reuters he is "absolutely" considering withdrawing from NATO, rebuking European allies for "lackluster support" of the Iran campaign. An American journalist was kidnapped in Iraq by suspected Iranian-backed militants. Thousands of additional U.S. troops are heading to the region. Moody's recession model is 1 percentage point from a signal that has never been wrong — and that was before the war.

**Headlines Across Sources:**
- [SpaceX Said to File Confidentially for IPO Before AI Rivals](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-01/spacex-is-said-to-file-confidentially-for-ipo-ahead-of-ai-rivals) — Bloomberg
- [Trump Claims Iran Wants a Ceasefire, Iran Says Remarks Are 'False and Baseless'](https://www.washingtonpost.com/national/2026/04/01/iran-israel-us-lebanon-latest-april-1-2026/e5de1fa4-2d7f-11f1-aac2-f56b5ccad184_story.html) — Washington Post
- [Wall Street Surges as Peace Signals Emerge: Trump Forecasts End to Iran Conflict Within Weeks](https://markets.financialcontent.com/stocks/article/marketminute-2026-4-1-wall-street-surges-as-peace-signals-emerge-trump-forecasts-end-to-iran-conflict-within-weeks) — MarketMinute

- [Current Price of Oil as of April 1, 2026](https://fortune.com/article/price-of-oil-04-01-2026/) — Fortune

- [S&P 500 Rockets on End of War Hopes](https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/04/01/stock-market-live-april-1-2026-sp-500-spy-rockets-on-end-of-war-hopes/) — 24/7 Wall St
- [Nasdaq Climbs 1%; SpaceX Files for Highly Anticipated IPO](https://www.thestreet.com/latest-news/stock-market-today-apr-1-2026-updates) — TheStreet
- [BofA: Soaring Energy Prices to Push Headline Inflation to Nearly 4% YoY](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/31/stock-market-today-live-updates.html) — CNBC

**STOCK BRIEFING — END OF DAY**

**META ($578.64, +1.1%)** — Meta rode the broad tech bid higher. The AI infrastructure narrative remains the throughline — Meta's capex commitments position it well if the ceasefire narrative holds and growth expectations reset higher. Watch for any commentary on ad revenue trends given the consumer resilience data today.

**GOOGL ($294.37, +2.4%)** — Strong session for Alphabet, outperforming mega-cap peers. The stock pushed toward $296 intraday before settling at $294.37. Search and cloud remain solid pillars — the OpenAI funding round is a competitive headline but Google's distribution moat is underappreciated by consensus.

**AMZN ($210.57, +1.1%)** — Amazon gained on the risk-on rotation and better-than-expected retail sales data. AWS growth trajectory and the logistics cost story remain the key drivers. The stock has been a relative underperformer in this rally — look for mean-reversion opportunities if the ceasefire narrative holds.

**NFLX ($95.25, -0.4%)** — Netflix was a rare red name in a green tape. The stock continues to digest its post-split levels and faces questions about ad-tier monetization pace. Down ~55% from its 52-week high — the valuation reset may be overdone if subscriber growth holds.

**TJX ($159.51, +1.5%)** — Off-price retail continues to shine. TJX is the best-positioned name in the consumer trade-down thesis that accelerates in a war-driven inflationary environment. Retail sales beat today is a tailwind for traffic trends.

**BURL ($326.90, +2.6%)** — Burlington surged alongside the off-price cohort. The stock is approaching its February all-time high of $332.20. Momentum is real here — the trade-down consumer story has legs even if the economy weakens.

**ROST ($219.95, +1.2%)** — Ross rounded out the off-price trifecta with solid gains. CFO recently sold $1M+ in shares — insider selling is worth noting but isn't necessarily bearish at these levels given the stock's run.

**RDDT ($136.18, +1.1%)** — Reddit continues to build on its post-IPO base. The ad monetization story is gaining traction and the platform's unique position in AI training data gives it optionality that isn't fully priced.

**PINS ($18.25, -0.5%)** — Pinterest drifted lower despite the risk-on session. Down 42% over the past year — the ad headwinds from tariff-driven consumer spending concerns are real. Needs a catalyst to break out of the downtrend.

**DIS ($96.56, +0.3%)** — Disney barely participated in the rally. Down 25% from its summer peak, the stock is weighed by linear TV erosion, rising content costs, softer international park visitation, and the collapsed $1B OpenAI partnership. The risk/reward is getting interesting but there's no near-term catalyst.

**UBER ($71.93, +0.7%)** — Uber inched higher with the broad market. The $70 level has been a reliable floor — Trefis calls it "a steal" at current levels. Ride-hailing demand should benefit from any ceasefire-driven economic normalization.

**SHOP ($118.62, -0.8%)** — Shopify slipped despite the broader tech bid. The stock has pulled back 25% in 2026 on growth deceleration fears and tariff headwinds weighing on SMB merchant sentiment. The April 2 opening at ~$113 suggests more selling pressure ahead.

**SPOT ($484.91, +1.2%)** — Spotify posted decent gains. The stock has corrected 38% from its $785 52-week high — the premium content and podcast ad monetization story is intact but pricing power questions linger.

**SNAP ($4.60, +0.5%)** — Snap held its gains from Monday's 12% Irenic Capital activist surge. At $4.60, the stock is priced for failure — but activist involvement from Irenic demanding "bold changes" could be the catalyst for a re-rating if management executes.

**ETSY ($49.98, +0.6%)** — Etsy ticked higher modestly. The marketplace is caught between trade-down traffic benefits and GMV headwinds from discretionary spending pullbacks. Valuation is compressed enough to warrant a close watch.

**W ($75.50, +2.2%)** — Wayfair bounced hard with the consumer discretionary cohort. The stock has rallied 144% off its 52-week low of $20.41 — the home furnishings cycle is inflecting but RH's guidance miss tonight suggests caution on the high end.

**LYFT ($13.34, +1.0%)** — Lyft gained modestly. The stock trades at a fraction of its IPO price and remains a speculation on ride-hailing profitability. The $9.66 52-week low looks safe for now.

**V ($300.55, -0.6%)** — Visa was one of the day's underperformers, slipping on the session. Cross-border travel volumes could face headwinds if the Iran conflict persists. The $293.89 intraday low is the level to watch.

**WSM ($183.50, +0.9%)** — Williams-Sonoma gained modestly after strong Q4 results (EPS $3.04 vs. $2.91 est., same-store sales +3.2%). The RH guidance miss tonight could create relative value opportunities — WSM's execution has been far more consistent.

**WMG ($24.10, +0.4%)** — Warner Music was quiet, ticking slightly higher. The Revelator acquisition adds B2B music platform capabilities. The 3.16% dividend yield provides downside support.

**MDB ($248.13, +2.8%)** — MongoDB was one of the stronger tech names, pushing to $246.47 intraday high. The AI/database tailwind is real — enterprise workloads are scaling and MDB's document model is well-positioned for agentic AI applications.

**CART ($38.50, +2.8%)** — Instacart rallied with the delivery/consumer tech cohort. The stock is finding a base after its post-IPO drift and grocery delivery demand remains structurally elevated.

**DASH ($148.01, -0.2%)** — DoorDash was flat to slightly lower. The stock has been under pressure — down from $285 52-week high — as profitability questions resurface. The April 2 bounce to $151 suggests buyers are stepping in at these levels.

**LYV ($150.00, +0.9%)** — Live Nation gained modestly. Live events demand remains robust but the stock has pulled back ~2.4% over the past week. Any ceasefire-driven consumer confidence boost would be a tailwind for concert and event spending.

**NON-CONSENSUS IDEAS — EVENING UPDATE**

**1. Off-Price Retail Triple (TJX/BURL/ROST) — Long Thesis Strengthening**
Today's retail sales beat (+0.6% vs. +0.5%) and the off-price trio's outperformance confirm the trade-down thesis. With BofA projecting headline inflation to nearly 4% YoY from energy costs, the consumer squeeze accelerates and off-price wins wallet share. TJX at $159 with momentum, BURL approaching ATH at $332 — these are not just defensive plays, they're offensive positions in a war economy. The RH miss tonight (-18% after hours) underscores the bifurcation: luxury home is suffering, value retail is thriving.

**2. Gold Reversal Play — Long GLD/GDX**
Gold's +2.27% bounce to $4,785 off the bear market correction is significant. JPMorgan's $6,300 year-end target gives 32% upside. Real yields are falling (10Y at 4.30%, down from 4.44%), the dollar is weakening, and central bank accumulation hasn't slowed. The 21% correction from the January $5,595 high was a positioning flush — today's move suggests the floor is in. If Trump's address disappoints on ceasefire, gold runs to $5,000 by end of April.

**3. Semi Rebound — Long SMH (Selective)**
Intel +8.78% and Micron +8.96% signal the semi cycle isn't dead. Intel's $14.2B Fab 34 buyback from Apollo is a confidence signal on its 18A node. The "quiet supply crisis" narrative — where traditional processors become the bottleneck for agentic AI — is gaining traction. This is a tactical trade, not a structural position — but the sector's been beaten up enough that the risk/reward skews positive into any ceasefire scenario.

**4. SpaceX IPO Ecosystem — Watch for Satellite/Space Adjacencies**
SpaceX's confidential IPO filing targeting a $1.75T valuation and up to $75B raise is the biggest capital markets event of the year. Starlink at 9.2M subscribers and $10B+ revenue anchors the valuation. The June timeline means investor briefings start this month. Watch for a halo effect on space-adjacent names and a broader IPO market reopening signal — if SpaceX can price, the window is open for OpenAI and Anthropic.

**5. Ceasefire Fade Trade — Short Oil (Tactical)**
If Trump's 9 PM address credibly signals de-escalation, WTI breaks below $95 and Brent tests $98. Energy stocks would give back another 3-5%. But this is a high-conviction, short-duration trade — Iran's denial means the ceasefire narrative could unravel just as quickly. Use tight stops.

**WOODCOCK TREND SCOUT**

Brand Breakdown: Anthropologie (7), Free People (5)

Key Trend Observations: Heavy concentration in romantic/feminine tops — eyelet, embroidery, lace, and flutter sleeves dominate the Anthropologie assortment. Free People is leaning into statement lace and corset silhouettes. The $98-$198 price range signals the premium contemporary tier is still investing in decorative detail. Denim corset from Free People is a crossover trend worth watching for Juniors translation. No Juniors or Girls finds this run — scout coverage was Missy-only today.

**OVERNIGHT POSITIONING**

Watch Trump's 9 PM address — this is the single biggest variable for overnight markets. If the tone is de-escalatory, expect Asian markets to gap higher (Nikkei +2-3%, Hang Seng +1-2%) and crude to test $95 WTI. If Trump escalates or the Pezeshkian counter-message is defiant, oil snaps back above $105 and the VIX reopens above 26. European markets open with limited catalysts beyond Iran headlines. Key overnight data: China Caixin Manufacturing PMI (already out, watch for revisions), Eurozone Manufacturing PMI finals. The NATO withdrawal headline adds a new tail risk for European defense/bank names. Position for volatility, not direction — this is a two-way tape until the geopolitical picture clarifies.

**ACTION ITEMS**

1. **Watch Trump's 9 PM ET address** — Have your oil and VIX trades queued. If tone is dovish, add to off-price longs and consider short oil.
2. **Monitor Iran's Pezeshkian counter-address** — The back-and-forth messaging will determine overnight sentiment. Any hint of actual negotiations sends crude sub-$95.
3. **Review RH -18% after-hours** — The luxury home furnishings miss creates relative value in WSM. Consider adding WSM vs. short RH as a pair trade.
4. **SpaceX IPO tracker** — Investor briefings start this month. Begin mapping the satellite/space supply chain for secondary plays.

title: Evening Briefing — Thursday, April 2, 2026
content:
**Good Evening, Jazzy — Thursday, April 2, 2026**

Volatility ruled the tape today as Trump's prime-time Iran address sent futures reeling overnight, but a late-session recovery on Oman-brokered Strait of Hormuz diplomacy pulled the S&P and Nasdaq green by the close. Oil near $110 is the elephant in every room — and pharma tariff headlines landed in the final minutes to add another layer of uncertainty.

**TODAY'S MARKET WRAP**

**US Markets (Close):** S&P 500 closed at 6,582.69, +0.11% — a remarkable intraday reversal from being down 1.5% at the lows. Nasdaq Composite settled at 21,879.18, +0.18%, also recovering from a 2.2% intraday deficit. Dow Jones finished at 46,504.67, -61.07 pts (-0.13%), underperforming as industrials and consumer staples lagged. Russell 2000 rallied to 2,512.37, +0.64%, outperforming large-caps as small-cap domestic names benefited from the "America-first" energy narrative. VIX closed at 24.54, elevated but off intraday highs near 28 — the fear gauge is pricing continued uncertainty but the late fade suggests hedges are being monetized.

**What Moved:** The session was defined by the Iran war overhang. Trump's Wednesday night address offered no timeline for Strait of Hormuz reopening and pledged 2-3 more weeks of strikes. Futures cratered overnight, with Dow futures down 600+ points pre-open. The reversal came around 1:30pm ET when Iranian state media reported Tehran is working with Oman on a protocol to "monitor" ships through Hormuz — markets read this as a de-escalation signal. Energy was the clear sector winner (+3-4%). Tech held up on the recovery, while consumer discretionary was mixed. Defensives (utilities, healthcare) saw early-session safe-haven flows but gave back gains. In the final minutes, the Trump admin announced 100% tariffs on pharma imports unless manufacturers cut prices or produce domestically — a late volatility injection. Volume ran about 15-20% above the 20-day average, reflecting the headline-driven churn. Tesla dropped 5%+ on Q1 delivery miss (358K vs 366K consensus). Nike slid 1% after a wave of analyst price target cuts.

**Treasuries & Rates:** 10-year Treasury at 4.31%, 2-year at 3.79%. The 10s-2s spread held at +52bps, steepening slightly as the long end priced in inflation risk from elevated oil. The curve is telling you the bond market sees sustained energy-driven inflation pressure. No Fed commentary today, but the market is repricing — June cut probability has dropped meaningfully this week.

**Oil & Commodities:** WTI crude settled near $109.50, up nearly 10% on the session after briefly touching $113 intraday. Brent was around $111.69. The Strait of Hormuz remains functionally closed — traffic down 90% since the conflict began Feb 28. Iran has established a selective corridor near Larak Island, effectively monetizing its chokehold. Gold fell sharply, down 2.78% to $4,679, as the strong dollar and rising real rates offset haven demand. Copper and industrial metals were mixed.

**After-Hours Movers:** Relatively quiet post-close given the drama during regular hours. Pharma names saw some movement on the 100% tariff headline. Coinbase (COIN) got a lift after receiving conditional OCC approval to operate as a trust bank. SpaceX IPO filing (confidential S-1 filed April 1) continued to dominate chatter — $1.5T valuation target would make it the largest IPO ever.

**Geopolitical Update:** This is the dominant macro variable right now. Day 33 of the US-Iran conflict. Trump's address to the nation Wednesday night was hawkish — pledging to strike Iran "extremely hard" for 2-3 more weeks with no clear endgame for Hormuz. He told allies to "build some delayed courage" and go "take" their own oil — a remarkable statement that rattled European and Asian allies. The afternoon recovery came on the Oman mediation headline, but the fundamental situation is unchanged: Hormuz traffic down 90%, oil up 60%+ since the war began, and no ceasefire framework in sight. The GCC Secretary-General called for UN Security Council authorization to use force to protect Hormuz — an escalation in diplomatic rhetoric. Iran is playing a double game: blocking most traffic while running a selective corridor through Larak Island to maintain its own export revenue. This is a classic squeeze — and until it breaks, oil stays bid.
